title: Password Basics
description: Test your understanding of strong passwords, password reuse, password managers, and safe password habits.

choices.text: They make computers run faster
They help protect accounts, systems, and sensitive information
They replace antivirus software
They are only used for email accounts

questions.explanation: Passwords are one of the first lines of defense for protecting systems and sensitive information.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a strong password practice?

choices.text: Using your pet's name because it is easy to remember
Using the same password for every account
Using a long, unique password for each account
Writing your password in an unprotected text file

questions.explanation: Strong passwords should be long and unique for each account.

choices.text: A short PIN used for printers
A longer password made of multiple words
A security question answer
A default system password

questions.explanation: A passphrase is a longer password made up of multiple words and is often easier to remember while still being strong.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a risky real-world password habit?

choices.text: Using a password manager
Creating a unique password for each account
Using a pet's name or birthday as a password
Changing a compromised password quickly

questions.explanation: Attackers often try common personal information such as pet names, birthdays, and simple patterns.

questions.prompt: Why is reusing the same password across multiple accounts dangerous?

choices.text: It makes your browser slower
It can allow one breach to affect multiple accounts
It prevents you from logging in
It disables password managers

questions.explanation: If one reused password is exposed in a breach, attackers may try it on other accounts.

choices.text: Storing employee IDs in a spreadsheet
Automatically trying stolen usernames and passwords on many accounts
Adding more complexity symbols to a password
Resetting all passwords after a software update

questions.explanation: Credential stuffing is when attackers use stolen login credentials from one breach to try accessing other accounts.

questions.prompt: What is one major benefit of a password manager?

choices.text: It eliminates the need for all passwords
It stores and helps generate unique strong passwords
It makes all accounts use the same password
It shares passwords with coworkers automatically

questions.explanation: Password managers help users generate and store strong, unique passwords for different accounts.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the best response to the challenge of remembering many passwords?

choices.text: Reuse one easy password everywhere
Write all passwords on a sticky note
Use a password manager and enable MFA when available
Use only your birthday with different symbols

questions.explanation: Password managers and MFA are practical ways to improve security while accounting for real human behavior.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a good way to protect your password?

choices.text: Email it to yourself for backup
Share it with trusted coworkers
Keep it unique and avoid sending it through messages or email
Use the same password for all work systems

questions.explanation: Passwords should not be shared or sent through insecure channels. Unique passwords reduce risk.

questions.prompt: What should you do first if you think your password has been compromised?

choices.text: Wait and see if anything happens
Change the password immediately
Keep using it until IT contacts you
Post a warning to social media

questions.explanation: If a password may be compromised, changing it immediately is the most important first step.
